metadata.title: Short Introductions to Electronics
metadata.author: Engineering Lecture Notes
metadata.description: All 31 short introductions to electronics covering fundamental electronic concepts, circuit analysis, and semiconductor devices.

content.text: Title slide for Lecture 02: Electrical Parameters
A Short Introduction to Electrical Parameters
Prerequisites: Newtonian Physics
Core definitions of charge and current using the hydraulic analogy
Charge and Current
Charge (Q) is analogous to water in pipes — measured in coulombs (C). One coulomb is the charge on 1.6×10⁻¹⁹ electrons.
Current is the rate of flow of charge: Q = I × t

image.alt: Hydraulic analogy of a closed-loop circuit

content.text: Voltage as pressure, resistance as pipe narrowness; Ohm's Law introduced
Voltage and Resistance
Voltage (V) acts like pressure — charge moves from high to low voltage. Energy equation: W = V × Q
Resistance limits current flow: V = I × R (Ohm's Law)

image.alt: Pump and narrow pipe showing resistance

content.text: Capacitance as elastic membrane barrel; inductance as propeller analogy
Capacitance and Inductance
Capacitance: Q = C × V — stores voltage/energy, not net charge.
Inductance: e = L × dI/dt — opposes changes in current. Analogy: a heavy propeller with inertia.

image.alt: Barrel with elastic membrane representing a capacitor

content.text: Summary of key electrical parameters
Summary — Electrical Parameters
• Charge (Q): measured in coulombs; Q = It
• Voltage (V): measured in volts; W = VQ
• Current (I): measured in amps; one amp = one coulomb per second
• Resistance (R): V = IR; measured in ohms
• Capacitance (C): Q = CV; measured in farads
• Inductance (L): e = L dI/dt; measured in henries
Title slide for Lecture 03: Ohm and Kirchhoff's Laws
A Short Introduction to Ohm's and Kirchhoff's Laws
Prerequisites: Electrical Parameters
Ohm's Law with node-based formulation; V = IR and current direction conventions
Ohm's Law
V = IR — current through a resistor is proportional to voltage across it.
Node form: V_A − V_B = I_AB × R. Positive current flows from higher to lower potential.

image.alt: Two-node resistor diagram showing current and voltage directions

content.text: Kirchhoff's Current Law: sum of currents at a node equals zero
Kirchhoff's Current Law (KCL)
The total current flowing into any node equals the total current flowing out. Charge cannot be created or destroyed.

image.alt: Node with four current branches illustrating KCL

content.text: Kirchhoff's Voltage Law: sum of voltage drops around any closed loop is zero
Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL)
The sum of all voltage differences around any closed loop is zero.
Limitation: breaks down with changing magnetic fields through a circuit loop (Faraday's law).

image.alt: Simple circuit loop illustrating KVL

content.text: Summary of the three fundamental DC circuit laws
Summary — Ohm and Kirchhoff's Laws
• Ohm's Law: V = IR (valid for linear resistors)
• KCL: Σ currents into node = 0
• KVL: Σ voltages around loop = 0
• All three laws are approximate — work best for small, low-frequency DC circuits.
Title slide for Lecture 04: Resistor Networks
A Short Introduction to Resistor Networks
Prerequisites: Ohm and Kirchhoff's Laws
Series and parallel resistor combinations and the potential divider
Series and Parallel Resistors
Series: R_total = R1 + R2 + ... (same current, voltages add)
Parallel: 1/R_total = 1/R1 + 1/R2 + ... (same voltage, currents add)

image.alt: Series and parallel resistor network diagrams

content.text: Potential divider formula and current divider
Potential and Current Dividers
Potential divider: V_out = V_in × R2 / (R1 + R2)
Current divider: I1 = I_T × R2 / (R1 + R2)

image.alt: Potential divider circuit with two resistors

content.text: Title slide for Lecture 05: Linearity and Superposition
A Short Introduction to Linearity and Superposition
Prerequisites: Ohm and Kirchhoff's Laws, Resistor Networks
Definition of a linear circuit and why superposition applies
Linearity
A circuit is linear if scaling all inputs by k scales all outputs by the same k. Resistors, capacitors, and inductors are linear components.
Diodes are non-linear — the superposition principle does not apply directly.
Superposition theorem: analyse each source independently then sum results
Superposition Theorem
The response due to multiple independent sources is the sum of responses due to each source acting alone.
Procedure: set all other voltage sources to 0 V (short circuit) and current sources to 0 A (open circuit), solve, then sum.

image.alt: Circuit split into two source-only sub-circuits for superposition analysis

content.text: Title slide for Lecture 06: Thévenin and Norton Theorems
A Short Introduction to Thévenin and Norton's Theorems
Prerequisites: Ohm's Law, Kirchhoff's Laws, Resistor Networks
Thévenin equivalent: any linear two-terminal network → voltage source + series resistor
Thévenin's Theorem
Any linear two-terminal network can be replaced by a single voltage source V_TH in series with a resistor R_TH.
V_TH = open-circuit voltage; R_TH = V_OC / I_SC (or found by zeroing all sources).

image.alt: Real network and its Thévenin equivalent circuit

content.text: Norton equivalent: current source + parallel resistor; relationship to Thévenin
Norton's Theorem
Any linear two-terminal network can also be replaced by a current source I_N in parallel with R_N.
I_N = I_SC; R_N = R_TH = V_OC / I_SC.

image.alt: Norton equivalent circuit with current source and parallel resistor

content.text: Determining Thévenin equivalent in the lab using V vs I plot
Measuring Equivalent Networks in the Lab
Plot load voltage V against load current I — the gradient is −R_TH and the V-axis intercept is V_TH.
Avoids short-circuiting outputs, which may damage amplifiers.
Title slide for Lecture 07: Circuit Loading
A Short Introduction to Circuit Loading
Prerequisites: Thévenin and Norton Theorems
Effect of connecting a load to a circuit — voltage divider between source and load resistance
Circuit Loading Effects
When a load R_L is connected, the output voltage drops from V_OC to V_TH × R_L / (R_TH + R_L).
Low-output-impedance sources and high-input-impedance loads minimise loading.

image.alt: Source with Thévenin resistance driving a load resistor

content.text: Title slide for Lecture 08: DC Circuit Analysis
A Short Introduction to DC Circuit Analysis
Prerequisites: Ohm's Law, Kirchhoff's Laws, Resistor Networks, Linearity and Superposition
Nodal analysis algorithm: identify nodes, assign variables, apply KCL and Ohm's law, solve
Nodal Analysis
1. Identify all nodes. 2. Assign voltage variables. 3. Apply KCL at each non-fixed node. 4. Apply Ohm's/component law to each element. 5. Solve simultaneous equations.

image.alt: Potential divider circuit with labelled nodes and currents for nodal analysis

content.text: Source transformation and use of Thévenin/Norton equivalents to simplify analysis
Source Transformation Shortcuts
Ladder circuits can be analysed by repeatedly absorbing one component at a time via Thévenin↔Norton transformations.
Dependent sources: use nodal analysis — do NOT zero dependent sources during source transformation.
Title slide for Lecture 09: Electric and Magnetic Fields
A Short Introduction to Electric and Magnetic Fields
Prerequisites: Newtonian Physics
Coulomb's law, electric potential, and field vs potential distinction
Electric Fields and Potential
Coulomb's law: F = q1 q2 / (4πε₀ r²). Like charges repel; unlike attract.
Electric potential at distance r from charge q1: V = q1 / (4πε₀ r). Voltage difference = work per unit charge.

image.alt: Electric field flux lines radiating from an isolated positive charge

content.text: Parallel plate capacitor derivation from Gauss's law: C = ε₀ εᵣ A/d
Capacitors and Gauss's Law
Uniform field between infinite parallel plates: E = σ/ε₀. For a parallel-plate capacitor: C = ε₀ εᵣ A / d
Energy stored per unit volume in electric field: u = ε₀ E² / 2

image.alt: Two parallel plates with flux lines between them

content.text: Magnetic fields: Gilbert model, flux lines, permeability
Magnetic Fields
Isolated magnetic monopoles do not exist — magnets always appear as dipoles (North and South).
Magnetic force law: F = μ₀ s1 s2 / (4π r²). Energy density: u = B² / (2μ₀).

image.alt: Flux lines around a magnetic dipole

content.text: Title slide for Lecture 10: Electrons in Solids
A Short Introduction to Electrons in Solids
Prerequisites: Newtonian Physics, Electric and Magnetic Fields
Energy bands in solids — conductor, semiconductor, insulator from Fermi-Dirac distribution
Energy Bands and Fermi-Dirac Distribution
Electrons fill energy states from the bottom up. The Fermi-Dirac distribution: p(E) = 1 / (e^((E−μ)/kT) + 1).
Conductors: continuous states at Fermi level. Insulators: large band gap. Semiconductors: small gap.

image.alt: Energy band diagrams for conductor, semiconductor, and insulator

content.text: n-type and p-type doping of semiconductors; p-n junction and depletion region
Doping and p-n Junctions
n-type: extra electrons (Group V dopant, e.g. phosphorus). p-type: fewer electrons / holes (Group III, e.g. boron).
At p-n junction: built-in potential from charge diffusion creates a depletion region. Forward bias → conducts; reverse bias → blocks.

image.alt: Energy band diagram of a forward-biased p-n junction

content.text: Drude model explains finite resistance: electron collisions with nuclei produce R ∝ L/A
Drude Model of Resistance
Electrons accelerate in electric field then scatter off nuclei. Average drift gives R = 2mₑ L / (n A q² τ).
Resistance increases with length and decreases with cross-sectional area — consistent with Ohm's Law.
Title slide for Lecture 11: Electromagnetism
A Short Introduction to Electromagnetism
Prerequisites: Newtonian Physics, Electric and Magnetic Fields
Biot-Savart law: current produces circular magnetic field; right-hand screw rule
Biot-Savart Law
A current element I·ΔL produces a magnetic field H = (μ₀/4π) × I sin θ / r². Right-hand screw rule gives direction.

image.alt: Diagram showing current element and resulting circular magnetic field

content.text: Faraday's law of electromagnetic induction: EMF = −dΦ_B/dt; Lenz's law
Faraday's Law and Inductors
A changing magnetic flux through a circuit induces an EMF: ε = −N A × dB/dt.
Inductor equation: V_A − V_B = L × dI_AB/dt. Energy stored: E = ½LI².

image.alt: Coil of wire with changing magnetic flux and induced EMF

content.text: Transformers: two coupled coils; voltage ratio = turns ratio; Maxwell's equations predicting EM waves
Transformers and EM Waves
Two coupled coils share magnetic flux. Voltage ratio = N1/N2. Power is transferred without direct electrical connection.
Maxwell (1864) predicted electromagnetic waves at 3×10⁸ m/s — consistent with the measured speed of light.
Title slide for Lecture 12: Diodes
A Short Introduction to Diodes
Prerequisites: Ohm and Kirchhoff's Laws, Resistor Networks
Shockley diode equation; I-V characteristic; forward voltage ~0.65 V for silicon
The Shockley Equation
I = I₀ (e^(V/nkT) − 1). For silicon diodes: forward voltage drop ≈ 0.6–0.7 V at typical currents.
Reverse saturation current I₀ is strongly temperature-dependent. Ideality factor n ≈ 1–2.

image.alt: I-V characteristic of a typical silicon diode showing forward and reverse regions

content.text: Static vs dynamic resistance; non-linear behaviour; iteration technique for circuit solving
Resistance and Non-Linearity
Static resistance: Rs = V/I. Dynamic resistance: Rd = dV/dI — more relevant for small-signal analysis.
Circuits with diodes cannot be solved analytically — use numerical iteration.
Zener diodes: defined reverse breakdown voltage; used as voltage references
Zener Diodes
Engineered to break down at a defined reverse voltage (e.g. 3.3 V). Used as voltage references.
Piecewise-linear approximation: I = (V + V_Z) / R_D in breakdown region.

image.alt: Zener voltage reference circuit with series resistor

content.text: Title slide for Lecture 13: Op-Amps
A Short Introduction to Op-Amps
Prerequisites: Ohm and Kirchhoff's Laws, Resistor Networks
Ideal op-amp properties: infinite gain, infinite input impedance, zero output impedance
The Ideal Op-Amp
Key properties: infinite open-loop gain (A → ∞), infinite input impedance, zero output impedance.
Golden rules for linear operation: (1) V+ = V− (virtual short); (2) I_in = 0.

image.alt: Op-amp circuit symbol with inverting and non-inverting inputs

content.text: Inverting and non-inverting amplifier configurations; gain formulae
Inverting and Non-Inverting Amplifiers
Inverting amplifier gain: A_v = −R_f / R_in
Non-inverting amplifier gain: A_v = 1 + R_f / R_in

image.alt: Inverting op-amp amplifier circuit with feedback and input resistors

content.text: Title slide for Lecture 14: Linear Op-Amp Circuits
A Short Introduction to Linear Op-Amp Circuits
Prerequisites: Op-Amps
Summing amplifier, difference amplifier, and voltage follower (buffer)
Summing, Difference and Buffer Circuits
Summing amplifier: V_out = −R_f × (V1/R1 + V2/R2 + ...). Difference amplifier: V_out = (V2 − V1) × R_f/R_in.
Voltage follower: A_v = 1, extremely high input impedance — used to buffer signals without loading.

image.alt: Summing amplifier with multiple input resistors and feedback

content.text: Title slide for Lecture 16: Real Components
A Short Introduction to Real Components
Prerequisites: Complex Impedance (for parasitics)
Resistor colour code; component tolerances; value markings on capacitors and inductors
Colour Codes and Tolerances
Resistor colour code: last digit is the multiplier (power of 10). Tolerance band: brown=1%, red=2%, gold=5%.
Capacitor markings use pF as default unit. Third digit is multiplier. Letter suffix gives tolerance (K=10%, M=20%).

image.alt: Resistor colour code chart showing digit, multiplier and tolerance colours

content.text: Equivalent networks for real resistors, capacitors, inductors showing parasitic L, C, R
Parasitics and Equivalent Networks
Real resistors: parasitic series inductance (~10 nH) and parallel capacitance (~0.05 pF). Limits usable range.
Real capacitors: ESR (series resistance) and series inductance. Self-resonant frequency: f₀ = 1/(2π√(LC)).
Real inductors: series resistance Rs; Q = ωL/Rs. Significant for filter design.

image.alt: Equivalent circuit models for real resistor, capacitor, and inductor

content.text: Title slide for Lecture 17: Time Variance and Time Constants
A Short Introduction to Time Variance and Time Constants
Prerequisites: Electrical Parameters, DC Circuit Analysis
RC discharge: exponential decay v(t) = V₀ e^(−t/RC); time constant τ = RC
RC Circuits — Charging and Discharging
Discharging: v(t) = V₀ e^(−t/τ), where τ = RC. After one time constant, voltage drops to 36.8% of initial value.
Charging: v(t) = Vs(1 − e^(−t/τ)). After one time constant, reaches 63.2% of final voltage.

image.alt: Exponential decay curve for RC capacitor discharge

content.text: RL circuits: time constant τ = L/R; integrator and differentiator op-amp circuits
RL Circuits and Op-Amp Integrators
Inductor time constant: τ = L/R. Cannot instantly change current through an inductor.
Op-amp integrator: V_out = −(1/RC) ∫ V_in dt. Differentiator: V_out = −RC × dV_in/dt.

image.alt: Op-amp integrator circuit with input resistor and feedback capacitor

content.text: Break frequency ω = 1/τ where amplitude is reduced by √2 and phase shift is 45°
Break Frequency
At ω = 1/RC: amplitude reduced by factor √2, phase shift = 45°. This is the break (corner) frequency.
For frequencies ≪ 1/τ: capacitor follows input. For frequencies ≫ 1/τ: capacitor voltage → 0, 90° phase shift.
Title slide for Lecture 18: Phasors
A Short Introduction to Phasors
Prerequisites: Time Constants, Electrical Parameters
Phasor representation: sinusoidal signals as rotating vectors in complex plane
Phasors
A sinusoidal signal V₀ cos(ωt + φ) is represented as a phasor: V = V₀ e^(jφ). Amplitude = magnitude, phase = argument.
Phasors simplify AC analysis by replacing differential equations with algebraic complex-number equations.

image.alt: Phasor diagram showing voltage and current vectors in the complex plane

content.text: Title slide for Lecture 19: Complex Impedance
A Short Introduction to Complex Impedance
Prerequisites: Phasors, Electrical Parameters
Impedance of R, C, L as complex numbers; Ohm's law generalised to V = IZ
Complex Impedance
Resistor: Z = R. Capacitor: Z = 1/(jωC). Inductor: Z = jωL.
Generalised Ohm's law: V = I × Z. Impedances combine as resistors (series: sum; parallel: product/sum).

image.alt: Impedance triangle showing R, X, and Z for a series RL circuit

content.text: Title slide for Lecture 20: AC Circuit Analysis
A Short Introduction to AC Circuit Analysis
Prerequisites: Phasors, Complex Impedance, DC Circuit Analysis
Applying nodal analysis and Thévenin/Norton theorems to AC circuits using complex impedances
AC Nodal Analysis
Replace all components with complex impedances and apply the same nodal analysis algorithm as DC.
Thévenin and Norton equivalents still apply — V_TH and Z_TH are now complex numbers.

image.alt: AC circuit with complex impedances and labelled nodes

content.text: Title slide for Lecture 21: Frequency Responses
A Short Introduction to Frequency Responses
Prerequisites: Complex Impedance, AC Circuit Analysis
Transfer function H(jω) = V_out/V_in; magnitude and phase as functions of frequency
Transfer Functions
Transfer function H(jω) = V_out/V_in. Magnitude |H(jω)| gives gain; arg(H(jω)) gives phase shift.
Low-pass, high-pass, band-pass, and band-stop responses arise from different RC/RL/LC topologies.

image.alt: Bode magnitude and phase plots for a first-order low-pass filter

content.text: Title slide for Lecture 22: Bode Plots
A Short Introduction to Bode Plots
Prerequisites: Frequency Responses, Complex Impedance
Bode plot construction: asymptotic approximations, −20 dB/decade slope per pole/zero
Bode Plot Construction
Plot 20 log|H(jω)| (dB) vs log(ω). Each pole contributes −20 dB/decade above its break frequency.
Each zero contributes +20 dB/decade. Phase: each pole adds −90° phase shift asymptotically.

image.alt: Asymptotic Bode magnitude plot for a two-pole low-pass transfer function

content.text: Title slide for Lecture 23: Op-Amp Amplifier Bandwidths
A Short Introduction to Op-Amp Amplifier Bandwidths
Prerequisites: Bode Plots, Op-Amps
Gain-bandwidth product (GBW); closed-loop bandwidth = GBW / closed-loop gain
Gain-Bandwidth Product
Real op-amps have a finite open-loop bandwidth. Gain-bandwidth product (GBW) = gain × bandwidth = constant.
For closed-loop gain A_cl, bandwidth ≈ GBW / A_cl. Higher gain → smaller bandwidth.

image.alt: Open-loop and closed-loop Bode plots showing gain-bandwidth product

content.text: Title slide for Lecture 24: First-Order Responses
A Short Introduction to First-Order Responses
Prerequisites: Frequency Responses, Time Constants
Step response of first-order RC system; exponential approach to final value with time constant τ
First-Order Step Response
For a step input, output: y(t) = y_final × (1 − e^(−t/τ)). Time constant τ = RC or L/R.
Rise time (10%→90%): t_r ≈ 2.2τ. Settling time to within 5%: t_s ≈ 3τ.

image.alt: First-order step response curve showing exponential rise to final value

content.text: Title slide for Lecture 25: Second-Order Frequency Response
A Short Introduction to Second-Order Frequency Response
Prerequisites: First-Order Responses, Frequency Responses
Second-order transfer function; natural frequency ω₀ and damping factor ζ; resonance peak
Second-Order Resonance
Standard form: H(s) = ω₀² / (s² + 2ζω₀s + ω₀²). Natural frequency ω₀ = 1/√(LC). Damping factor ζ.
For ζ < 1 (underdamped): resonance peak at ω₀. For ζ = 1/√2: maximally flat (Butterworth). For ζ > 1: overdamped.

image.alt: Second-order frequency response for various damping factors

content.text: Title slide for Lecture 26: Second-Order Step Response
A Short Introduction to Second-Order Step Response
Prerequisites: Second-Order Frequency Response
Underdamped, critically damped, overdamped step responses; overshoot and ringing for ζ < 1
Second-Order Step Response
Underdamped (ζ < 1): oscillatory response with overshoot. Critically damped (ζ = 1): fastest non-overshoot. Overdamped (ζ > 1): sluggish.
Overshoot (%) = 100 × e^(−πζ/√(1−ζ²)).

image.alt: Second-order step response for underdamped, critically damped, and overdamped cases

content.text: Title slide for Lecture 27: Active Filters
A Short Introduction to Active Filters
Prerequisites: Op-Amps, Second-Order Frequency Response, Bode Plots
Sallen-Key and multiple-feedback topologies; Butterworth and Chebyshev designs
Active Filter Topologies
Active filters use op-amps to achieve second (or higher) order filtering without inductors.
Sallen-Key topology implements low-pass, high-pass, or band-pass. Butterworth: maximally flat. Chebyshev: steeper roll-off with ripple.

image.alt: Sallen-Key low-pass active filter circuit with op-amp

content.text: Title slide for Lecture 28: Non-Linear Op-Amp Circuits
A Short Introduction to Non-Linear Op-Amp Circuits
Prerequisites: Op-Amps, Diodes
Comparator, Schmitt trigger (positive feedback adds hysteresis), precision rectifier
Comparators and Schmitt Triggers
Comparator: op-amp without feedback — output saturates high or low based on input polarity.
Schmitt trigger: positive feedback adds hysteresis — prevents noise-induced multiple switching.

image.alt: Schmitt trigger circuit and its hysteresis transfer characteristic

content.text: Precision half-wave rectifier using op-amp eliminates diode forward-voltage drop
Precision Rectifier
An op-amp in the feedback loop of a rectifier eliminates the 0.6 V diode drop, enabling rectification of small signals.

image.alt: Precision half-wave rectifier circuit with op-amp and diode in feedback

content.text: Title slide for Lecture 29: Non-Ideal Op-Amp Behaviour
A Short Introduction to Non-Ideal Op-Amp Behaviour
Prerequisites: Op-Amps, Linear Op-Amp Circuits
Input offset voltage, bias currents, slew rate, finite CMRR, finite open-loop gain
Non-Ideal Op-Amp Parameters
Key non-idealities: input offset voltage V_os, input bias current I_B, common-mode rejection ratio CMRR, slew rate SR.
Slew rate limits the maximum rate of output change: SR = ΔV_out/Δt (V/μs). Limits usable bandwidth at large amplitudes.

image.alt: Non-ideal op-amp model including offset voltage source and bias current sources

content.text: Title slide for Lecture 30: Power in AC Circuits
A Short Introduction to Power in AC Circuits
Prerequisites: AC Circuit Analysis, Complex Impedance
Real, reactive, and apparent power; power factor; RMS values
Real, Reactive, and Apparent Power
RMS voltage: V_rms = V_peak / √2. Real power P = V_rms × I_rms × cos φ (watts). Reactive power Q = V_rms × I_rms × sin φ (VAR).
Apparent power S = V_rms × I_rms (VA). Power factor = P/S = cos φ. Unity power factor for purely resistive loads.

image.alt: Power triangle showing P, Q, S and phase angle φ

content.text: Title slide for Lecture 31: Noise
A Short Introduction to Noise
Prerequisites: AC Circuit Analysis, Op-Amps
Thermal (Johnson) noise, shot noise, flicker (1/f) noise — sources and power spectral density
Types of Noise
Thermal (Johnson) noise: V_n² = 4kTRΔf. Present in all resistors at temperature T. White noise (flat spectrum).
Shot noise: I_n² = 2qIΔf. Caused by discrete nature of charge carriers. Flicker (1/f) noise dominates at low frequencies.

image.alt: Noise power spectral density showing thermal, shot, and 1/f contributions

content.text: Signal-to-noise ratio (SNR), noise figure, noise temperature; cascaded stages (Friis formula)
SNR, Noise Figure, and Friis Formula
SNR = P_signal / P_noise (or expressed in dB). Noise figure NF = 10 log(SNR_in / SNR_out).
Cascaded stages (Friis): NF_total = NF1 + (NF2−1)/G1 + .... First stage dominates — use a low-noise amplifier first.
Title slide for Lecture AA: Frequency, Phase, and Amplitude
A Short Introduction to Frequency, Phase, and Amplitude
Prerequisites: Electrical Parameters
Sinusoidal signals: amplitude, frequency, period, phase; angular frequency ω = 2πf
Sinusoidal Signal Parameters
A sinusoid: v(t) = V₀ cos(ωt + φ). Amplitude = V₀; angular frequency ω = 2πf; period T = 1/f; phase = φ.
Peak-to-peak = 2V₀. RMS = V₀/√2 for a sinusoid.

image.alt: Sinusoidal waveform labelled with amplitude, period, and phase shift

content.text: Phase difference between two signals; leading vs lagging; measurement in degrees or radians
Phase Relationships
Two signals at the same frequency: v1(t) = V₁ cos(ωt), v2(t) = V₂ cos(ωt + φ). If φ > 0, v2 leads v1.
Phase is only meaningful for signals at the same frequency. Represented in phasor diagrams as an angle.

image.alt: Two sinusoidal waveforms showing a phase difference of 90 degrees
